title: CHA DC423 :: Canvas, Classrooms, and the Cost of Over-Reliance
author: tothehilt
content: >> Field Notes: When the Learning Platform Becomes the Single Point of Failure

The recent Canvas-related hacking headlines land right in the middle of several current conversations: ransomware hitting school districts, MFA fatigue attacks, third-party app sprawl, AI tools in classrooms, and the growing habit of putting every critical workflow behind one cloud login. It is a reminder of something security people have been saying for years: convenience is not resilience.

Schools adopted learning management systems because they solve real problems. Assignments are easier to distribute. Grades are easier to track. Parents, teachers, and students can communicate from the same place. That is all useful. The problem starts when the platform stops being a tool and quietly becomes the classroom itself.
[01] Digital Does Not Mean Durable
•	If a grading portal, assignment system, or messaging platform goes down, class should not grind to a halt.
•	If one compromised account can disrupt instruction or expose sensitive data, the process needs more safeguards.
•	If students cannot function without the app, we have trained dependency instead of capability.
[02] Security Is Also a Curriculum Issue

A school system can buy software, but it cannot outsource judgment. Students should understand passwords, phishing, MFA fatigue, privacy, backups, and why screenshots live forever. Teachers and administrators need the same training, not as a once-a-year compliance video, but as normal operational hygiene.
[03] Current Topics Are Connected
•	AI in schools is useful, but it also creates new privacy, cheating, hallucination, and over-trust problems.
•	Ransomware crews know education is time-sensitive; if class, payroll, buses, and grades all depend on the same network, pressure goes up fast.
•	Third-party integrations make platforms powerful, but every plugin, OAuth grant, and forgotten vendor account expands the blast radius.
•	Cloud platforms reduce local maintenance, but they also make identity security the new front door. One phished account can become a hallway pass.
[04] Paper Still Has a Place

This is not a call to throw laptops in the river. Technology belongs in schools. But analog backups matter. Printed instructions, offline reading, whiteboards, in-person discussion, and locally saved lesson plans are not old-fashioned. They are graceful degradation. When the network breaks, learning should continue.
[05] Practical Recommendations
•	Require MFA for staff and administrative accounts, and teach users what real MFA prompts look like.
•	Keep offline copies of critical lesson plans, rosters, and emergency procedures.
•	Limit permissions. Not every account needs access to everything.
•	Audit third-party integrations and old accounts before attackers do.
•	Practice a no-platform day so teachers and students know how to operate when systems fail.
•	Teach digital literacy as a survival skill, not an elective afterthought.
[06] The Bigger Lesson

A hacked platform is a security incident. A school that cannot teach without that platform is a resilience incident. The goal is not less technology. The goal is better balance: use the tools, understand the risks, and keep enough human process alive that one outage or compromise does not own the day.
Hack responsibly. Teach resiliently.
EOF
